result:
The repair shop opened on a Tuesday because Mondays, even for imaginary things, tend to be brittle. I painted the sign by hand, because machines don’t know where to leave room for mercy. It said, Repairs, reasonable and unreasonable. Walk in with what you can carry. Walk out with what you can live with.

By noon the bell had announced its first customer. A man in a suit entered, holding his smile in both hands as if it might spill. It was lopsided, too much show on the right. He laid it gently on the counter. “I have meetings,” he said, “and this won’t sit on my face the way it used to.”

I took out the blue cloth I use for faces and spread the smile upon it. Under good light, you could see where the habit had worn through to the hope beneath. “Do you clench or swallow?” I asked. “Both,” he admitted. I squared the edges, loosened the hinge where sincerity connects to performance, and stitched in two tiny breath-holes. “Try this,” I said. He fitted the smile. It didn’t gleam; it rested. He nodded; he could tell. “What do I owe?” he asked.

I charge in minutes. A repair takes time and returns time, so it’s a clean currency. He gave me six: two from a conference call that wouldn’t miss him, two from a commute he’d surrender to birdsong, and two from the end of a day when he would refuse to argue with a microwave. I put the minutes in the jar beside the register. They clicked together like little gray shells.

Some days the shop is a parade of small, sharp breaks. The single sock whose pair didn’t disappear so much as defect to a life of freedom. The spoon that tastes like Thursdays. I keep a drawer of common fixes: spare dawns in case you wake and your light won’t start, a bottle of glue that only works after you admit you need it, a fistful of clean silences for people who were talked over so often that their vowels came unthreaded.

Other days, the brokenness is complicated. A woman came in with a paper bag. She upended it onto the counter. A lullaby slid out, dull as a coin worn smooth. “My father doesn’t sleep,” she said. “I tried singing. It works on the dog. It used to work on him.”

I tuned the lullaby to match his age. There are harmonics that emerge when you begin to forget doors and keep only the rooms. I roughed up a corner of the melody, so it could snag on the ear. I pulled two notes sideways to make space for the memory of hands washing dishes in a rivulet afternoon. “He’ll still wake,” I warned her. “But he’ll land in softer grass.” She nodded. People don’t come for miracles. They come for mercies that don’t require an explanation.

Once a boy brought me his shadow. It was sulking, heavy with summer and swinging from his fist. “It left early,” he said accusingly. “Before the fireworks. I want it to stay next time.”

“You can’t make a shadow stay,” I told him. “You can only give it reasons to return.” I washed it in a basin of dusk so it would take dirt honestly. I tied to its edge a bell so fine it could only ring when nobody else was listening. “When you’re bored,” I said, “take it for a walk around the block. And when it lengthens, don’t try to step on it. Let it lead.” He looked as if I’d betrayed some ancient game. A few days later his mother stuck her head in the door to say he was dragging chairs all over the house, making mountains for the shadow to climb. Not everything is a fix. Sometimes it’s a new way to play with what you already have.

A woman wearing the city on her shoulders pushed through one afternoon with a rolled map jammed under her arm. The map refused to stay rolled. It kept trying to spread itself over the counter, as if to escape. “It’s for the neighborhood plan,” she said. “Every time I fold it to show people the future, it unfolds to show me the present. I think it’s resisting.”

“Maybe,” I said, “but maps are supposed to resist until you ask the right question.” I warmed it with a lamp and rubbed its margins with a little rainwater from last April. Then I penciled in a bakery at a corner where someone would definitely decide to bake, and a bench nobody would notice until the day their feet needed it. The map lay down, finally, and rolled itself with a sigh. Consent is a kind of hinge.

The strangest repairs are metaphoric. A poet came in carrying a phrase. “I’m drowning in paperwork,” he gasped, holding the phrase above his head as if it could breathe. It was soggy with complaint. “You’re not drowning,” I told him. “You’re stuck in a tidepool.” I drained the phrase into a pan, picked the shells of shame out of it, and returned it with a small question tucked in its pocket: If the sea is language, are you afraid of the words or the water? He left muttering something better that might grow into a line.

I don’t fix everything. A woman with a jacket that smelled like the last cigarette of her ex-boyfriend asked me to erase it. “Not the memory,” she said. “Just the smell.” I told her I could only move it, not remove it. We tucked the scent into the lining where the wrist meets the world, and she agreed to learn when to roll her sleeves down and when to shove them up.

Sometimes I think customers mistake the shop for a chapel. They tell me secrets as if I had absolution folded in the till with the receipts. It’s just a counter. It’s just a small room full of labeled boxes and unlabeled burdens. Still, I keep a little pew in the corner, and sometimes, at the end of the day, I sit where others have rested and try to fix my own unfixables.

I have a hairline crack where certainty used to be. It passes for wisdom in good light, but at night it whistles. On the second shelf, behind a jar of errant commas, there’s a map with no north. It was given to me by a person I loved who never told me the name of the town they came from. I repair other people’s direction while mine wavers like a moth. So far, it’s an honest waver. I let it be.

A man came in last week with a sentence he almost said. It was still warm from his mouth, complicated by what he thought the other person would have heard. He wanted me to turn it into a sentence that would work. “What does work mean?” I asked. “That I won’t be alone,” he said. I set the sentence under the lamp, peeled back its implication, and rolled out the wish that was hiding inside. In the end, I sent him away with a simpler version and a measure for timing: say it in the doorway, with your shoes still on. Leave them room to go, and yourself room to stay.

People ask if it’s exhausting, all this mending of the invisible. It isn’t. It’s like sweeping: repetitive, occasionally satisfying, full of quiet revolutions. Also, there are tips. The jar beside the minutes is for stories. Now and then, someone thanks me by leaving a memory I didn’t earn. I never spend those. I keep them in a drawer lined with tissue paper, and on the rare mornings when my shop key sticks and my hands feel large and clumsy, I open the drawer and borrow a childhood I never had. The one with the dog that knew when you were sad and presented you with a nose. Or the one with the older sister who didn’t accuse, she simply knew. I wear those for an hour and return them unwrinkled.

At closing, I sweep the floor for the day’s crumbs of almost and indecision. I count the minutes in the jar and add my own: three, from when I didn’t check my phone in line; two, from standing in the alley watching steam loosen winter from the rooftops; one, saved from talking myself out of a walk. I flip the sign to Closed and underline it, lightly, with a pencil. The pencil is always dull. It makes the line look less like an order and more like an intention.

On the door, taped at a child’s height, there’s a note scrawled in a green marker that hardly works. It says, If you find something and you don’t know if it’s yours, it might be mine. Bring it here. Draw a picture if you can’t carry it. I leave a box of crayons under it, always missing two. People add to the page: a wobbly moon, a key without a door, a cat with seven whiskers on one side and none on the other. A whole gallery of the lost, recruiting its own rescuers. If a repair can’t happen on the counter, sometimes it happens on the glass.

Tomorrow it will be Tuesday again for someone, no matter what the calendar says. I will unlock. The bell will ring like a thought remembering itself. And the day will walk in, carrying what it can.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
